meta.title: Bhagavad Gita — Ekam Sat Node Taxonomy v2
meta.description: Exhaustive extraction of all doctrinal propositions from the BG (BORI Critical Edition). Every verse maps to at least one node. Pre-sectarian lens.
meta.methodology: Bottom-up verse-by-verse proposition extraction, clustered by distinct claims, validated for completeness
meta.hk_note: Terms given in Harvard-Kyoto for grep against BORI corpus (BORI ch = BG ch + 22)
meta.lens: Pre-sectarian (Ekam Sat, RV 1.164.46). Nodes stated in BG's own language, not through any later darshana.

nodes.name: Dharma-Sankata Frame — The Crisis at Kurukshetra
nodes.proposition: The battlefield of dharma is the site where the entire epic's moral tension crystallizes; Arjuna's refusal names the crisis
nodes.repetition_rationale: Frame verse 1.1 — the single opening that names the entire epic's tension; BG returns to dharma-crisis at 2.7 and 18.72
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for passages where a warrior or king names a moral impasse using dharma-sankata language before a sage intervenes with teaching

nodes.name: Moral Paralysis — Visada as Entry Condition
nodes.proposition: Arjuna's grief and bodily collapse at the sight of kin is the necessary prior condition for all teaching that follows
nodes.repetition_rationale: First and only occurrence of the collapse itself; echoed structurally whenever a hero in MBH weeps before receiving wisdom
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Scenes where a warrior prostrates in grief and a teacher responds with instruction — the viSAda-upadesha pattern

nodes.name: Kulakshaya Cascade — Destruction of Family Destroys Dharma
nodes.proposition: When lineages are destroyed, eternal family-dharmas perish, women are corrupted, varna-samkara arises, and ancestors fall
nodes.repetition_rationale: Arjuna's own argument, used ironically — BG does not endorse it but the MBH narrative takes it seriously as a social concern
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Passages in AdiP and StrIparva where lineage-destruction and its dharmic consequences are mourned

nodes.name: Armies and Conch-Blowing — The Epic Stage
nodes.proposition: The cataloguing of warriors and the cosmic sound of conch-shells establishes war as a cosmic event, not a personal quarrel
nodes.repetition_rationale: The first half of ch1 — narrative setup that makes Arjuna's collapse more striking
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Battle catalogues and conch-blowing formulae across MBH war narratives

nodes.name: The Wise Grieve Not — Rebuke of Conventional Grief
nodes.proposition: Krishna rebukes Arjuna's grief as unworthy of a wise person; the learned grieve neither for the living nor the dead
nodes.repetition_rationale: Stated at 2.11 (opening rebuke) and restated at 2.30 after the full atman argument; grief-rebuke is the BG's rhetorical opening
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH scenes where a sage explicitly tells a grieving hero that grief is unbecoming the wise; contrast with legitimate grief in StrIparva

nodes.name: Indestructibility of the Self — Atman is Unborn and Undying
nodes.proposition: The atman is unborn, eternal, undying, ancient — it cannot be slain when the body is slain
nodes.repetition_rationale: BG's foundational metaphysical argument — stated, expanded, and restated across 2.12-2.30; the most elaborated node in chapter 2
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Any MBH passage using aja/nitya/avyaya to describe the self or Brahman; especially philosophical discourses in Shantiparva

nodes.name: Body-Self Distinction — Dehin and Deha
nodes.proposition: The embodied self (dehin) passes through childhood, youth, and old age in the body (deha); death is merely change of garment
nodes.repetition_rationale: Repeated to anchor each phase of the atman argument: dehin-deha at 2.13, antavanta-dehe at 2.18, garment metaphor at 2.22
nodes.echo_hypothesis: The garment/body metaphor for death in MBH narrative; kings and sages using zarIra-dehin language in philosophical asides

nodes.name: Tolerance of Opposites — Titiksha
nodes.proposition: Sense-contacts bring pleasure and pain as cold and heat — they are impermanent; the wise one must endure them
nodes.repetition_rationale: First statement; elaborated later as sama/equanimity in 2.38, 2.48, 6.7; titiksha is a distinct virtue — endurance before equanimity
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH descriptions of kshatriya or muni enduring extremes — tapas in heat and cold, svarga descriptions using zItoSNa language

nodes.name: Sat and Asat — Being and Non-Being
nodes.proposition: Non-being has no existence; being has no non-existence — the boundary of both is seen by knowers of truth
nodes.repetition_rationale: Single verse but philosophically the densest — the ontological foundation; echoed in 13.12, 17.26 (sat/asat usage in different registers)
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Philosophical asides in MBH where sat/asat are used as technical ontological terms, particularly in Shantiparvan moksha-dharma

nodes.name: Ascharya — The Marvellous Nature of the Self
nodes.proposition: Some see the self as a marvel, some speak of it as a marvel, some hear of it as a marvel — yet having heard, none truly know it
nodes.repetition_rationale: Single aphoristic verse; its rhetorical form (fourfold parallel) suggests it is a pre-existing wisdom saying being cited
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages using Azcaryavat about the self or the divine — this may be a floating formula across parvas

nodes.name: Svadharma and the Warrior's Duty
nodes.proposition: For a kshatriya, no greater good exists than righteous battle; such a war comes of itself as an open gate to heaven
nodes.repetition_rationale: Stated at 2.31 as duty-argument and reinforced at 2.33 as consequence-argument; structurally a two-step persuasion
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where sages or elders invoke svadharma and kshatra-dharma to persuade hesitant warriors before battle

nodes.name: Equanimity in Success and Failure — Samatvam
nodes.proposition: Yoga is equanimity; perform actions established in yoga, having abandoned attachment, equal in success and failure
nodes.repetition_rationale: 2.38 pairs victory/defeat; 2.48 defines yoga itself as samatva — the definition is given after its application
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH sages and kings described as sama in victory and defeat; especially Yudhishthira's post-war equanimity discourse

nodes.name: Buddhi Yoga — Single-Pointed Resolute Intelligence
nodes.proposition: The resolute intelligence is one-pointed; the irresolute has infinite branching thoughts; buddhi yoga alone frees from karma-bondage
nodes.repetition_rationale: 2.41 defines the single vs. many-branched intelligence; 2.44 shows distracted intelligence cannot settle in samadhi; 2.50 identifies buddhi yoga as skill in action
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages contrasting the resolute and irresolute mind; discourses on buddhi as distinct from manas in Shantiparva

nodes.name: Transcendence of Vedic Ritual — Beyond the Three Gunas
nodes.proposition: The Vedas deal with the three-guna domain; be beyond the three gunas, free from pairs, ever in sattva, not seeking acquisition
nodes.repetition_rationale: BG critiques the vedavadins — those who think Vedic ritual exhausts the spiritual; the well-simile at 2.46 is the summary
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where karma-kanda ritual is contrasted with jnana or moksha paths; Shantiparva debates on ritual vs. knowledge

nodes.name: Sthitaprajna — Portrait of the Steady-Minded Sage
nodes.proposition: The sthitaprajna has abandoned all desires from the mind, is content in the self alone, unmoved in sorrow, not exulting in pleasure, free of passion, fear, and anger
nodes.repetition_rationale: The entire 2.54-72 section is a sustained portrait; four distinct criteria repeated: desire, emotions, senses, and ocean-simile; brAhmIsthiti at 2.72 is the culminating term
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH descriptions of rishis or kings using these exact qualities — vItarAga, vItabhaya, vItakrodha as a formulaic triad; especially Bhishma's descriptions of sages in Shantiparva

nodes.name: Desire-Chain — From Thought to Ruin
nodes.proposition: Contemplating sense-objects generates attachment; from attachment arises desire; from desire anger; from anger delusion; from delusion memory-loss; from that destruction of intelligence; from that the person perishes
nodes.repetition_rationale: Single two-verse sequence but one of the most quoted in the entire BG; a causal chain — re-echoed at 3.37-41 in expanded form
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages (especially Yaksha-prashna, Shantiparva) where the causal sequence of kama-krodha-moha is explicitly stated

nodes.name: Nishkama Karma — Act Without Attachment to Fruit
nodes.proposition: You have a right only to action, never to its fruits; let not fruit be your motive; let not inaction be your refuge
nodes.repetition_rationale: 2.47 is the core verse; 3.19 restates for the karma-yoga argument; 4.20 gives the liberated-while-acting form; this is the BG's most repeated practical teaching
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages — particularly Janaka's teaching, Vyasa's discourses — on acting without desire for fruit

nodes.name: Compulsory Action — No One Can Be Without Action
nodes.proposition: Not even for an instant can anyone exist without performing action; all are compelled to act by the gunas of prakriti
nodes.repetition_rationale: 3.5 states the compulsion; 3.8 restates as prescription — do the necessary action; the argument counters the sannyasa-as-inaction misunderstanding
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH philosophical passages arguing against pure inaction; debates in Shantiparva about whether the sannyasin truly ceases all action

nodes.name: Yajna as Cosmic Reciprocity — The Wheel of Sacrifice
nodes.proposition: Creation was joined with sacrifice by Prajapati; by this shall you prosper; this is the wish-granting cow; the wheel thus set in motion must be kept turning
nodes.repetition_rationale: 3.10 introduces cosmic yajna; 3.14-15 gives the anna-parjanya-yajna-karma-brahman chain; 3.16 names non-participation as sin
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH cosmological passages linking yajna, rains, food, and cosmic order; creation myths in Adiparva where Prajapati establishes sacrifice

nodes.name: Lokasangraha — Acting for the Welfare of the World
nodes.proposition: The wise person should act for the welfare of the world without attachment, having regard for the cohesion of peoples
nodes.repetition_rationale: 3.20 gives the Janaka example; 3.21 states the general principle; 3.25 contrasts attached and unattached action for the same social goal
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where a king or sage cites responsibility to the world as motive for action despite personal exhaustion

nodes.name: Gunas Act — Not the Self — False Doership
nodes.proposition: In all cases it is the gunas of prakriti that act; one deluded by ego thinks 'I am the doer'
nodes.repetition_rationale: 3.27 diagnoses the delusion; 3.28 gives the corrective view of the tattvavit; elaborated again at 5.8-9 and 13.29
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Shantiparva moksha-dharma passages where the sage explicitly denies doership

nodes.name: Desire as the Enemy — Kama the Great Devourer
nodes.proposition: It is desire, it is anger, born of rajas, all-devouring, greatly sinful — know this as the enemy here
nodes.repetition_rationale: 3.37 names desire-anger; 3.38 gives three metaphors (smoke/fire, dust/mirror, membrane/embryo); 3.43 concludes with the hierarchy
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH moral discourses explicitly naming kama as enemy using mahAzana or duSpUra imagery

nodes.name: Sense-Desire Hierarchy — Intellect Above Senses
nodes.proposition: Above the senses is mind; above mind is intellect (buddhi); above intellect is the Self — knowing the Self above intellect, subdue the self by the Self
nodes.repetition_rationale: Culminating verse of ch. 3; the hierarchy recurs in Sankhya framework across BG; echoed at Katha Upanishad 3.10-11
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Sankhya-style hierarchies in MBH philosophical discourses listing sense-mind-buddhi-atman

nodes.name: Guru-Parampara — The Eternal Teaching Passed Down
nodes.proposition: This imperishable yoga was proclaimed to Vivasvat, by Vivasvat to Manu, by Manu to Ikshvaku — the royal seers thus knew it, but it was lost by time
nodes.repetition_rationale: Sets the BG within cosmic time and lineage — this teaching is not new; positions it as recovery of lost wisdom
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages citing the Manu-Ikshvaku lineage; passages where an ancient teaching is said to have been lost and recovered

nodes.name: Avatara — Divine Incarnation to Restore Dharma
nodes.proposition: Whenever dharma declines and adharma rises, the divine manifests age after age for protection of the good and destruction of the wicked
nodes.repetition_rationale: The divine descent as response to dharmic crisis — which is precisely the situation of the MBH itself
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where Krishna's incarnation purpose is stated or acknowledged by other characters

nodes.name: Knowing Divine Birth Liberates
nodes.proposition: One who truly knows the divine birth and action, having shed the body does not return to rebirth
nodes.repetition_rationale: The soteriological payoff of the avatara doctrine — knowledge of divine birth itself liberates
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where knowing Krishna's true nature is described as liberating

nodes.name: All Paths Lead to Me — Reciprocal Divine Response
nodes.proposition: In whatever way people approach the divine, the divine rewards them in that way — all paths are valid
nodes.repetition_rationale: One of the most universalist declarations in BG — all paths are valid; distinct from bhakti-exclusivism
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages affirming the validity of multiple paths to the same goal

nodes.name: Varna is by Guna-Karma — Not Birth
nodes.proposition: The fourfold varna-system was created according to guna and karma — not by birth
nodes.repetition_rationale: Varna defined by guna/karma not birth — the BG's implicit critique of birth-based caste
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where varna is attributed to guna/karma rather than jati; Vanaparva debates and Shanti-Anushasana on Brahmin qualities

nodes.name: Action in Inaction — The Paradox of the Free Actor
nodes.proposition: One who sees inaction in action and action in inaction is wise among humans — a yogi who has accomplished all action
nodes.repetition_rationale: 4.18 states the paradox; 4.19-22 give three modes of the free actor
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where a sage acts in the world but is explicitly said not to be bound — Janaka narratives

nodes.name: Jnana-Yajna — Knowledge as the Highest Sacrifice
nodes.proposition: As fire reduces fuel to ash, the fire of knowledge reduces all actions to ash; no purifier equal to knowledge exists
nodes.repetition_rationale: 4.24 (brahmarpanam), 4.33 (jnana-yajna supremacy), and 4.37 (fire metaphor) form a triptych
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages using jnana-agni or jnana-yajna as a metaphor; fire of knowledge burning karma in sage biographies

nodes.name: Seek the Teacher — Guru-Seva for Knowledge
nodes.proposition: Know that by prostration, question, and service; the knowers of truth will teach you knowledge
nodes.repetition_rationale: The guru-shishya protocol stated explicitly; echoed throughout MBH as the standard opening of philosophical dialogues
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH philosophical discourse scenes using the praNipAta-pariprazna-sevA triad

nodes.name: Doubt as the Root of Ruin
nodes.proposition: The doubt-natured one perishes; for the doubting self there is neither this world nor the next nor happiness
nodes.repetition_rationale: Both the diagnosis (doubt destroys) and the cure (cut doubt with sword of knowledge) in a single passage
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where a hero's doubt is identified as the root problem — notably Yudhishthira's recurring doubts

nodes.name: Sannyasa and Yoga Lead to the Same End
nodes.proposition: Both renunciation and yoga lead to the highest good; of the two, karma yoga is superior to renunciation without yoga
nodes.repetition_rationale: 5.2 asserts both paths lead to highest good; 5.4-5 states Sankhya and yoga see the same
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH debates between sannyasins and active warriors about which path is superior; Suka-Janaka dialogue

nodes.name: The Non-Doer in Action — Senses Move Among Senses
nodes.proposition: The yogi who knows truth thinks 'I do nothing at all'; the senses move among sense-objects like a lotus leaf untouched by water
nodes.repetition_rationale: 5.8-9 give the exhaustive list of actions performed without doership; 5.10 gives the lotus-leaf metaphor
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH descriptions of sages active in the world but untouched — Janaka, Shuka — using lotus and water metaphors

nodes.name: Brahma-Nirvana — Extinction in Brahman
nodes.proposition: The sage with inner joy, inner delight, inner light, being Brahman, attains brahma-nirvana
nodes.repetition_rationale: brahmanirvANa appears at 5.24, 5.25, 5.26 — three consecutive verses; the term bridges Vedantic and Buddhist vocabulary
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages using brahmanirvana or brahmabhuta as the goal of the sage

nodes.name: Samadarshin — Equal Vision Across All Beings
nodes.proposition: The pandits see with equal eye the Brahmin, the cow, the elephant, the dog, and the dog-eater
nodes.repetition_rationale: The most radical social statement in ch. 5 — equal vision includes the outcaste; directly enacts ekam-sat
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where a sage is praised for treating all beings equally; Yaksha-prashna; Chandala episodes

nodes.name: Krishna as Enjoyer of All Sacrifice — Sarvalokamaheshvara
nodes.proposition: Having known Krishna as the enjoyer of all sacrifices, the Lord of all worlds, the friend of all beings, one attains peace
nodes.repetition_rationale: Ch. 5 culminating verse — synthesizes the three roles: receiver of yajna, ruler of all worlds, friend of all
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH hymns addressing Krishna or Vishnu using these three epithets

nodes.name: Sannyasa Equals Yoga — No Yoga Without Renouncing Sankalpa
nodes.proposition: What they call sannyasa, know that as yoga; no one becomes a yogi who has not abandoned sankalpa
nodes.repetition_rationale: Ch. 6 opens by collapsing the sannyasa/yoga distinction — the real renunciation is of intention
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH debates on true vs. false sannyasa

nodes.name: Self as Both Friend and Enemy
nodes.proposition: Let the self elevate the self; let not the self depress the self; the self alone is the self's friend, the self alone the self's enemy
nodes.repetition_rationale: The reflexive self-relationship — not an external enemy but the untamed mind itself is the obstacle
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages on jita-atman and the inner enemy; descriptions of ideal kings and sages

nodes.name: The Yogi's Portrait — Sama in All Conditions
nodes.proposition: The yogi who sees clod, stone, and gold as equal, is steady, sense-controlled — said to be truly integrated
nodes.repetition_rationale: The kuta-stha portrait — unmoved as a peak — alongside the extreme equality (clod = stone = gold)
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH character descriptions using samaloSTa-ashma-kanchana or kuta-stha as praise of sages

nodes.name: Dhyana Method — The Actual Practice of Meditation
nodes.proposition: The yogi should practice seated, in a clean place, holding body neck and head erect, gazing at the nose-tip, mind controlled, established in brahmacharya
nodes.repetition_rationale: The only fully systematic description of meditation posture and technique in the BG; prescriptive not descriptive
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH ascetic descriptions using the same technical posture vocabulary; Vanaparva tapas descriptions

nodes.name: Mind as the Restless Enemy — Abhyasa and Vairagya
nodes.proposition: The mind is restless, turbulent, powerful — yet by practice and dispassion it is restrained
nodes.repetition_rationale: Arjuna's doubt articulates the universal problem; Krishna's answer (abhyasa + vairagya) is the complete practical prescription
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages using the formula abhyasa-vairagya as the two-limbed path

nodes.name: The Fallen Yogi — Continuity Across Births
nodes.proposition: The yogi who falls from yoga is not lost — reborn in a pure or wise family, the previous practice carries forward
nodes.repetition_rationale: The reassurance to the incomplete yogi — effort is never wasted across births
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages on rebirth carrying forward spiritual merit; stories of sages reborn with memory of previous attainment

nodes.name: The Yogi is Supreme — Above Ascetics, Scholars, and Ritualists
nodes.proposition: The yogi is superior to ascetics, scholars, and ritualists — therefore be a yogi
nodes.repetition_rationale: A ranking verse — BG places yoga above tapas, jnana, and karma separately
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages ranking paths — tapas vs. jnana vs. yajna vs. yoga; Shantiparva comparative debates

nodes.name: Bhakta-Yogi as the Highest Yogi
nodes.proposition: Among all yogis, the one devoted with inner self gone to Me, worshipping with faith — that one I consider the most integrated
nodes.repetition_rationale: Ch. 6 culminating verse — the bhakta-yogi tops the hierarchy; bridges dhyana to devotion
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where a devotee-sage is ranked above mere ascetic or scholar

nodes.name: Vision of Self in All Beings — Sarvabhutastham Atmanam
nodes.proposition: The yogi established in oneness worships the divine abiding in all beings — sees the self in all and all in the self
nodes.repetition_rationale: 6.29-6.32 — four consecutive verses consolidating the ekam-sat vision; the clearest statement of the project's central thesis
nodes.echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where a sage sees the one in all — Shantiparva descriptions of brahmajna sages and Narayaniya section

nodes.name: Two Prakritis — Para and Apara
nodes.proposition: The divine has two natures: the lower eightfold material nature and the higher living nature (jivabhuta) that sustains the world
nodes.repetition_rationale: First statement of the two-nature doctrine. Ch9 and Ch13 develop it further.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for para/apara prakrti distinction in Sankhya passages, Moksha-dharma of Shantiparva

nodes.name: The Divine as Origin and Dissolution of All
nodes.proposition: Both natures together are the womb of all beings; the divine is the origin (prabhava) and the dissolution (pralaya) of the entire world
nodes.repetition_rationale: Stated again at 9.18, 10.8, 14.4 — BG insists that all emergence and dissolution trace to the one source
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Cosmogonic passages in Adiparva, Shanti-parva creation hymns

nodes.name: Nothing Higher Than the Divine — Beads on a Thread
nodes.proposition: There is nothing higher than the divine; all of this is strung on it as beads on a thread
nodes.repetition_rationale: Repeated in essence at 9.4-5 and 10.42. The string-of-beads metaphor is distinctive.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: String/thread metaphors for cosmic unity in MBH philosophical passages

nodes.name: The Divine Is the Essence in All Things
nodes.proposition: The divine is the taste in water, the light of sun and moon, the sacred syllable in the Vedas, sound in space, the vital force in all beings
nodes.repetition_rationale: Anticipates the vibhuti catalogue of ch10; here stated as inner essence (rasa) not outward glory
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Immanence language — divinity as essence of natural phenomena — in MBH hymns

nodes.name: The Three Gunas Originate from the Divine — But the Divine Transcends Them
nodes.proposition: The three-guna states all arise from the divine, yet the divine is not in them; the world, deluded by these three, does not know the divine
nodes.repetition_rationale: The asymmetric relation: gunas in the divine, not divine in gunas. Restated in 9.4-5.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Passages distinguishing the absolute from guna-bound existence in Shantiparva Mokshadharma

nodes.name: Maya as Divine and Difficult to Cross
nodes.proposition: This divine maya made of gunas is difficult to cross; only those who take refuge in the divine cross beyond it
nodes.repetition_rationale: Maya as divine (not inferior) power — repeated at 18.61. Only surrender crosses it.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Maya as divine delusion in Shantiparva, Indra-maya episodes in Adiparva

nodes.name: Four Types of Devotees — Arta Jijnasu Artharthi Jnani
nodes.proposition: Four kinds worship the divine: the distressed, the seeker of knowledge, the seeker of benefit, and the wise; the wise is highest
nodes.repetition_rationale: Typology of seekers unique to ch7. The jnani-as-atman-of-God claim (7.18) is the most radical.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Typologies of seekers in MBH — distressed devotees, philosophers, pragmatic worshippers

nodes.name: Vasudeva Is All — Rare Knowledge
nodes.proposition: After many births, the wise one who knows that Vasudeva is all takes refuge — such a great soul is exceedingly rare
nodes.repetition_rationale: The ekam sat proposition made explicit — vAsudevaH sarvam. The theological nucleus of the project.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Any passage where a sage declares the unity of all in one divine principle

nodes.name: Worship of Other Deities — Limited but Valid
nodes.proposition: Those whose knowledge is stolen by desires worship other gods, but even that worship reaches the divine — though the fruits are limited
nodes.repetition_rationale: Restated at 9.23-24. All worship is redirected to the one — with a soteriological hierarchy.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Debates on relative merit of worshipping different deities in MBH

nodes.name: The Divine Conceals Itself Through Yoga-Maya
nodes.proposition: The divine is not manifest to all, veiled by yoga-maya; the deluded world does not recognize the divine as unborn and imperishable
nodes.repetition_rationale: The epistemology of concealment — why the divine is not universally recognized.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Passages on divine concealment, disguise episodes in MBH

nodes.name: Duality-Delusion Veils All Beings at Birth
nodes.proposition: All beings are born into the delusion of duality arising from desire and aversion
nodes.repetition_rationale: The structural diagnosis of bondage: dvandva-moha at the moment of birth.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Birth-bondage passages, desire/aversion as the root of samsara in Shantiparva

nodes.name: Aksara Brahman — the Imperishable Supreme
nodes.proposition: The imperishable (akshara) is the supreme Brahman; its own nature is called the adhyatma; the creative impulse that produces beings is called karma
nodes.repetition_rationale: The technical vocabulary of ch8 — defining terms for cosmic levels.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Akshara Brahman as technical term in Upanishad-influenced sections of Shantiparva

nodes.name: Last Thought at Death Determines Rebirth
nodes.proposition: Whatever state of being one remembers at the moment of death, to that state alone one goes
nodes.repetition_rationale: The death-moment doctrine is repeated at 8.13 for the specific case of remembering the divine.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Death-moment teachings in Shantiparva, instructions to dying warriors in Drona/Karna parvas

nodes.name: OM as the Imperishable — Dying While Remembering
nodes.proposition: Uttering the single syllable OM, which is Brahman, remembering the divine, one who departs attains the highest goal
nodes.repetition_rationale: OM as the sonic form of the imperishable — specific yogic instruction at death.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: OM as salvific sound in MBH — dying sages who utter OM

nodes.name: The Great-Souled Do Not Return — Moksha as Non-Return
nodes.proposition: Having attained the highest perfection, the great-souled ones do not take rebirth in this impermanent home of suffering
nodes.repetition_rationale: The negative definition of liberation: non-return. Contrasted with all worlds up to Brahmaloka being subject to return.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Non-return teachings in Shantiparva Mokshadharma

nodes.name: Cyclic Cosmic Time — Brahma's Day and Night
nodes.proposition: Brahma's day spans a thousand yugas; his night another thousand; at dawn all beings emerge from the unmanifest, at nightfall they dissolve back
nodes.repetition_rationale: The kalpa-yuga cosmology — unique to ch8. Establishes cosmic time scale.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Yuga-cycle cosmology passages in Shantiparva, Adiparva creation narratives, Markandeya's teachings

nodes.name: Beyond the Unmanifest — The Eternal Unperishing Abode
nodes.proposition: Beyond the unmanifest there is another eternal unmanifest which does not perish when all beings perish — this is the highest goal
nodes.repetition_rationale: The metaphysical stratification: manifest -> unmanifest -> beyond-unmanifest.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Three-level ontology passages in Shantiparva — vyakta/avyakta/akshara discussions

nodes.name: The Two Paths — Light and Dark (Uttarayana and Daksinayana)
nodes.proposition: Fire, light, day, bright fortnight, northern sun — departing this way one goes to Brahman; smoke, night, dark fortnight, southern sun — one returns
nodes.repetition_rationale: The two-paths doctrine (devayana/pitriyana) is ancient Vedic — BG appropriates it.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Devayana/pitriyana passages in Shantiparva and cosmological discourse

nodes.name: Rajavidya — The Royal Knowledge, Royal Secret
nodes.proposition: This knowledge is the royal knowledge, the royal secret, supremely purifying, directly known by experience
nodes.repetition_rationale: The self-designating claim of the BG as supreme knowledge.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Brahmavidya / rajavidya language in Shantiparva

nodes.name: God Pervades All Yet Does Not Dwell in Beings — Cosmic Paradox
nodes.proposition: All beings subsist in the divine yet the divine does not dwell in them — the great wind dwells in space yet space is not in the wind
nodes.repetition_rationale: The simultaneous immanence and transcendence paradox.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Philosophical passages on immanence without dependence in Shantiparva

nodes.name: God as Cosmic Creator Through Prakriti — Unmoved Mover
nodes.proposition: At the end of each kalpa all beings flow back into prakriti; at the beginning the divine sends them forth again — seated like an indifferent witness
nodes.repetition_rationale: God as unmoved first mover of cosmic cycles.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Passages on the unmoved mover of cosmic cycles in Shantiparva

nodes.name: The Foolish Mistake the Human Form
nodes.proposition: The deluded disregard the divine when it takes human form, not knowing its higher nature
nodes.repetition_rationale: The avatar-recognition problem — crucial for MBH narrative.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Episodes where Krishna's divinity is unrecognized — Duryodhana's rejection, Shishupala

nodes.name: The Divine Is All Cosmic Relations
nodes.proposition: The divine is father, mother, sustainer, grandfather, the way, supporter, lord, witness, dwelling, refuge, friend, origin, dissolution, ground, and imperishable seed
nodes.repetition_rationale: The total-relation litany: every possible relational category is claimed.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Prayers and hymns to the divine as all-relations in MBH

nodes.name: Svarga Through Ritual Is Temporary
nodes.proposition: Those who worship through Vedic ritual attain heaven — but when merit is exhausted they return to the mortal world
nodes.repetition_rationale: The critique of svarga-seeking religion: effective but impermanent.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Critiques of svarga-seeking in Shantiparva, Markandeya's discourses

nodes.name: Ananya Bhakti — Undivided Devotion, Divine Provides
nodes.proposition: Those who worship with undivided attention — the divine carries what they lack and preserves what they have
nodes.repetition_rationale: Yoga-kshema as divine promise to undivided devotees.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Divine protection of devotees in MBH — Draupadi's rescue, Prahlada

nodes.name: All Worship Reaches the Divine — One Recipient Behind All Gods
nodes.proposition: Even those who worship other gods with faith worship the divine alone, though by an improper method
nodes.repetition_rationale: All yajna has one true recipient. Foundational for the ekam sat thesis.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Universalist religious passages in MBH — Bhishma's teaching on one god behind many names

nodes.name: Even a Leaf Offered with Devotion Is Accepted
nodes.proposition: Whoever offers a leaf, flower, fruit, or water with devotion — the divine accepts that gift
nodes.repetition_rationale: The democratization of worship — any offering with pure devotion is received.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Stories of humble offerings accepted by the divine in MBH

nodes.name: Offer All Actions to the Divine — Madarpana
nodes.proposition: Whatever you do, eat, offer, give — do that as an offering to the divine; thus freed from karma-bonds
nodes.repetition_rationale: Karma-yoga distilled to its simplest: all action as divine offering.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Arpana language in MBH — any sage teaching that all action should be dedicated to the divine

nodes.name: Equal Vision — The Divine Is the Same Toward All
nodes.proposition: The divine is the same toward all beings; no one is hateful or dear — but those who worship with devotion are in the divine, and the divine is in them
nodes.repetition_rationale: Divine impartiality as baseline — but devotion creates reciprocal inhabitation.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Teachings on divine impartiality in Shantiparva

nodes.name: Even the Worst Sinner Is Saved by Devotion
nodes.proposition: Even the most sinful person who worships with undivided devotion should be considered righteous — my devotee does not perish
nodes.repetition_rationale: The radical soteriology of devotion — overcoming all karmic weight.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Redemption through devotion stories in MBH

nodes.name: All Beings Can Attain the Highest Goal
nodes.proposition: Even those of sinful birth — women, vaishyas, shudras — taking refuge in the divine, they too attain the highest goal
nodes.repetition_rationale: The universalism of liberation — across all social strata.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Liberation across social classes in Shantiparva — stories of shudras and women attaining moksha

nodes.name: The Divine Is the Origin of Gods and Great Seers
nodes.proposition: The seven great seers and the four Manus were born from the divine's mind — the world's progeny descend from them
nodes.repetition_rationale: The genealogy of cosmic authority — all teachers trace to the divine.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Seven sages and Manu lineages in MBH — Adiparva genealogies

nodes.name: All Qualities and States Arise from the Divine
nodes.proposition: Intelligence, knowledge, freedom from delusion, patience, truth — all arise from the divine alone
nodes.repetition_rationale: All categories — even opposites — originate from the divine. Anticipates vibhuti list.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Passages attributing all virtues to divine origin in Shantiparva

nodes.name: The Divine Is the Self in the Heart — Beginning Middle End
nodes.proposition: The divine is the self abiding in the heart of all beings; the beginning, middle, and end of all beings
nodes.repetition_rationale: The triple formula (adi-madhya-anta) is the most concentrated ekam sat formulation.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Adi-madhya-anta formula as divine epithet in MBH hymns

nodes.name: Vibhutis — Manifest Excellence as Divine Fragment
nodes.proposition: Whatever being has glory, grace, or power — know that to have sprung from a fragment of the divine's splendor; the divine pervades the world with a single portion
nodes.repetition_rationale: The entire vibhuti catalogue is one extended claim: excellence anywhere is a vibhuti.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Vibhuti lists and divine excellence enumerations in MBH

nodes.name: The Divine Gives Liberating Wisdom to Devotees
nodes.proposition: For those constantly united in devotion, the divine gives the yoga of understanding and destroys the darkness of ignorance with the lamp of knowledge
nodes.repetition_rationale: Divine grace as the final cause of liberating knowledge.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Divine grace enabling liberation in MBH — passages where divine intervention brings sudden clarity

nodes.name: Vishvarupa — The Entire Universe in One Body
nodes.proposition: Arjuna beholds in the divine body the entire world standing as one — all creation gathered at one point
nodes.repetition_rationale: A direct vision rather than inferential teaching. The visual datum for ekam sat.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Cosmic vision episodes in MBH — Sanjaya's vision, Narada's vision of Narayana

nodes.name: Divya Drishti — Only Devotion Reveals the Cosmic Form
nodes.proposition: The divine form cannot be seen with ordinary eyes; only by undivided devotion can it be known, seen, and entered
nodes.repetition_rationale: The epistemology of bhakti: only love reveals what intellect and austerity cannot.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Passages on special vision given by grace in MBH — Sanjaya's divya-drishti

nodes.name: Kala — The Divine as Time the Destroyer
nodes.proposition: The divine reveals itself as Time the destroyer — even without Arjuna, all warriors shall not exist; Arjuna is merely the instrument
nodes.repetition_rationale: The nimittamatra (mere instrument) claim reframes all human agency.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Kala-as-divine-destroyer passages in MBH — Dhritarashtra's laments, Markandeya's kala teachings

nodes.name: Terror and Devotion — Seeking the Familiar Form
nodes.proposition: Having seen the terrifying cosmic form, Arjuna begs to see the familiar form — the devotee needs an approachable form
nodes.repetition_rationale: The dialectic of the cosmic and the intimate: absolute terror answered by the comfort of the personal form.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Terror-to-comfort devotion arcs in MBH

nodes.name: Saguna Bhakti Is Easier for the Embodied
nodes.proposition: Those who worship the formless imperishable face greater hardship; the path through the personal divine is easier for embodied beings
nodes.repetition_rationale: The pragmatic hierarchy: saguna over nirguna for embodied seekers.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Debates on saguna vs nirguna paths in Shantiparva

nodes.name: Graduated Path — From Mind-Fixing to Fruit-Renunciation
nodes.proposition: If you cannot fix your mind, try abhyasa-yoga; if that fails, act for the divine; if that fails, renounce fruits; even this gives peace
nodes.repetition_rationale: The pedagogical descending ladder — BG's practical concession to different capacities.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Graded instruction passages in MBH

nodes.name: Fruit-Renunciation Surpasses Meditation
nodes.proposition: Knowledge is better than practice; meditation better than knowledge; renunciation of fruit is better than meditation — from renunciation peace immediately follows
nodes.repetition_rationale: A complete comparative ranking in a single verse.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Comparative hierarchies of spiritual practices in Shantiparva

nodes.name: Portrait of the Ideal Devotee
nodes.proposition: The ideal devotee is free of hatred, friendly, compassionate, without ego or possessiveness, equal in pain and pleasure, patient, content, self-restrained — such a one is supremely dear to the divine
nodes.repetition_rationale: The most extensive character portrait in BG — 8 verses defining the ideal devotee.
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Character descriptions of ideal sages in MBH — Bhishma's deathbed portrait of the wise

nodes.name: Twenty Items of Knowledge — Amanitvadi
nodes.proposition: True knowledge consists of twenty virtues beginning with humility (amānitva), non-hypocrisy, non-harm, patience, uprightness — these are the conditions for jñāna, not its content
nodes.repetition_rationale: First and only full enumeration of these twenty virtues as jñāna-conditions; the BG treats these as a unified set
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for amānitva, adhyātmanityatva, viviktadevasevitva as a cluster — any MBH sage or description using this vocabulary is echoing this node

nodes.name: Brahman as the Knowable — Anadi Para Brahma
nodes.proposition: Brahman is the object of knowledge — beginningless, called neither sat nor asat, pervading all, the light of lights, seated in the heart of all
nodes.repetition_rationale: Unique to ch13 — the fullest description of Brahman as the object of knowledge (jñeya), with paradoxical attributes
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for jñeya + brahma, anādi + brahma, jyotiṣām jyotiḥ — sages describing the knowable absolute in paradoxical terms

nodes.name: Purusha-Prakriti Duality — Both Beginningless
nodes.proposition: Purusha and Prakriti are both beginningless; Prakriti is the cause of effects and instruments, Purusha is the experiencer of pleasure and pain
nodes.repetition_rationale: The purusha-prakriti framework appears in ch3 and ch7 but ch13 gives the fullest Sāṃkhya-inflected account, with the witness-self portrait (13.22-23) as a sub-claim
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for puruṣa + prakṛti + anādi, bhoktṛtva paired with kartṛtva — Sāṃkhya passages in Śāntiparva and Anugītā will echo this

nodes.name: Liberation Through Vision — Seeing the Same Lord in All
nodes.proposition: One who sees the supreme Lord dwelling equally in all perishable beings, the imperishable within the perishable — that one truly sees
nodes.repetition_rationale: Restates N09 (same self in all) from a different angle — the vision of the imperishable in the perishable; the non-doer in the doer; these are three distinct but related perceptions converging on liberation
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for paśyati + samaṃ + sarvabhūteṣu, or the sky/ākāśa metaphor (like space not being stained) — this simile appears in Upaniṣads and may recur in MBH

nodes.name: Brahman as the Womb of All Beings
nodes.proposition: The great Brahman is the womb; the Lord places the seed in it — all beings are born from this union of the field and the knower
nodes.repetition_rationale: Unique cosmogonic image — Brahman as the cosmic womb, Ishvara as the father-seed — establishes the origin of diversity from a single source
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for mahad brahma + yoni, bīja + garbha in cosmogonic contexts — creation hymns and Brahma-creation narratives in Ādi and Śāntiparva

nodes.name: How Each Guna Binds the Embodied Self
nodes.proposition: Sattva binds through attachment to happiness and knowledge; rajas binds through attachment to action; tamas binds through delusion, sloth, and sleep
nodes.repetition_rationale: While N06 names the three gunas, this node is a distinct claim: the mechanism of bondage differs for each guna; even sattva binds
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for nibadhnāti + guṇa, sattvena badhnāti — Sāṃkhya expositions in Śāntiparva on how the gunas produce bondage

nodes.name: Cosmic Destinations by Dominant Guna at Death
nodes.proposition: At death, if sattva dominates one goes to higher worlds; if rajas, one is reborn among action-attached; if tamas, one is born in deluded wombs
nodes.repetition_rationale: The eschatological corollary of guna theory — where you go depends on which guna predominates at the moment of death; unique to ch14
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for ūrdhvaṃ gacchanti sattvasthāḥ + adho gacchanti tāmasāḥ — this trisection of cosmic destinations appears in Sāṃkhya contexts in Śāntiparva

nodes.name: Portrait of the Gunatita — Beyond the Three Qualities
nodes.proposition: One who neither hates illumination, activity, and delusion when present, nor desires them when absent, who is indifferent like a witness — that one has transcended the gunas
nodes.repetition_rationale: Parallel to the sthitaprajña portrait (N05) but now from the guna-framework angle; distinct because the criterion is not-hating-not-desiring the three qualities themselves
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for guṇātīta + udāsīna, sarvārambhaparityāgī — any sage described as unmoved by the three qualities, described from this vocabulary

nodes.name: Bhakti as the Vehicle Beyond the Gunas
nodes.proposition: By unswerving devotion one transcends the three gunas and becomes fit for Brahman — Brahman itself is the ground of the immortal, the eternal, the everlasting happiness
nodes.repetition_rationale: Ch14 closes with an unexpected move — after the entire guna analysis, it is bhakti (not jñāna or sāṃkhya) that lifts one beyond; unique synthesis here
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for bhaktiyogena guṇātīta — devotion framed as the means of transcending the guna-structure; rare formulation to track in MBH

nodes.name: The Cosmic Ashvattha Tree — World as Inverted Tree
nodes.proposition: The cosmic tree has its roots above and branches below — its leaves are the Vedas; one who knows it is a Veda-knower; cut it with the axe of non-attachment
nodes.repetition_rationale: The inverted tree is a unique cosmological image in BG — roots above = Brahman, branches below = manifest world; the whole world is a proliferating tree that must be cut
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for aśvattham + ūrdhvamūlam — this specific image appears in KaṭhaU 2.3.1 and may recur in Śāntiparva cosmological sections

nodes.name: The Jiva as Eternal Fragment of the Divine
nodes.proposition: The living soul in the world of life is an eternal fragment of Me — it draws to itself the mind and the five senses dwelling in Prakriti
nodes.repetition_rationale: Unique claim: the individual jīva is not separate from but is a fragment (aṃśa) of the divine — this is the metaphysical basis for devotion and oneness simultaneously
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for mamaivāṃśo jīvaloke — this exact phrase or its paraphrase; also jīvabhūta + sanātana in contexts of individual-divine relationship

nodes.name: The Lord as the Inner Power in All — Vaisvanara
nodes.proposition: The Lord enters the earth and sustains beings; becomes the fire (Vaiśvānara) in the body and digests the four kinds of food; enters the heart as memory and knowledge
nodes.repetition_rationale: Distinct from the antaryāmin node (N31): here the emphasis is on the Lord's identity with elemental cosmic functions — earth-sustainer, fire-digester, vedic knower — not just the inner witness
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for vaiśvānara + deha, smṛtir jñānam apohanaṃ ca — the Lord as digester and as the source of memory/forgetting; tracks across hymns to Viṣṇu/Brahman in MBH

nodes.name: Three Purushas — Kshara, Akshara, Purushottama
nodes.proposition: Two purushas exist in the world — the perishable (all beings) and the imperishable (the immovable kūṭastha); the highest is a third, the Purushottama who pervades and sustains all three worlds
nodes.repetition_rationale: This node refines N16 — while N16 names purushottama, this node provides the full tripartite ontology: kṣara (manifold becoming), akṣara (immovable being), and Purushottama (the transcendent who surpasses both)
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for kṣaraḥ sarvāṇi bhūtāni + kūṭastho 'kṣara — and the beyond-both formulation; Anugītā and Śāntiparva Sāṃkhya sections will deploy this triad

nodes.name: The Daivi Sampad — Divine Qualities as Liberation's Equipment
nodes.proposition: Fearlessness, purity of being, steadiness in jñāna-yoga, charity, self-restraint, sacrifice, study, austerity, uprightness — these constitute the divine estate that leads to liberation
nodes.repetition_rationale: While N30 contrasts daivi/āsuri natures, this node specifically maps the daivī sampad as soteriological equipment — a positive list of twenty-six virtues; it echoes the amānitva list of ch13 (N36) but extends it
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for abhayaṃ sattvasaṃśuddhi + daivīm — also any enumeration of virtue lists in MBH where sages describe the qualities of the good; these recur in Anuśāsanaparva

nodes.name: The Three Gates of Hell — Kama Krodha Lobha
nodes.proposition: Desire, anger, and greed are the three gates of hell — the destruction of the self; abandoning these three, one should act for one's own good
nodes.repetition_rationale: While N12 covers kāma as root of bondage, this is a distinct claim: the triple-gate formula — kāma-krodha-lobha as the three specific portals to self-destruction; this triad appears repeatedly in MBH
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for kāmaḥ krodhas tathā lobhas — this exact triad is one of the most grep-stable clusters; it appears verbatim in Śāntiparva and Anuśāsanaparva ethical sections

nodes.name: Shastra as the Authority for Action
nodes.proposition: One who abandons the injunction of śāstra and acts from personal desire attains neither perfection nor happiness nor the highest goal — let śāstra be your authority for what is to be done
nodes.repetition_rationale: Unique node: BG explicitly validates śāstra as the epistemological standard for action — this is the only place it makes this meta-claim about scriptural authority
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for śāstraṃ pramāṇaṃ — this formulation or śāstravidhim utsṛjya as a negative marker; dharmaśāstra-oriented passages in Anuśāsanaparva will echo this

nodes.name: Shraddha Traya — Three Types of Faith
nodes.proposition: Faith is of three kinds born of each person's own nature — the sattvic worship the devas, the rajasic worship yakṣas and rākṣasas, the tamasic worship pretas and bhūtas
nodes.repetition_rationale: The claim that a person is constituted by their śraddhā (yo yacchraddhāḥ sa eva saḥ) is among the most psychologically profound in BG; the tripartition by guna then applies this to worship
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for śraddhāmayo 'yaṃ puruṣaḥ — this is the most quotable formulation; also śraddhā + trividhā + svabhāvajā; track in texts on religious duty and devotional practice

nodes.name: Three Types of Tapas — Bodily, Speech, Mental
nodes.proposition: Austerity is of three kinds: of body (reverence, purity, non-harm), of speech (true, pleasant, beneficial words, recitation), and of mind (serenity, gentleness, silence, self-restraint, purity of intention)
nodes.repetition_rationale: While tapas is mentioned often in BG, this is the only systematic threefold analysis of tapas by mode (body/speech/mind) and by guna quality (sāttvika/rājasa/tāmasa)
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for śārīraṃ tapa + vāṅmayaṃ tapa + mānasam — this classification recurs in Anuśāsanaparva on tapas; also manaḥprasāda + saumyatva + mauna as a cluster

nodes.name: Three Types of Dana — Sattvic Rajasic Tamasic
nodes.proposition: Sattvic giving is to one who cannot repay, in the right place, time, and to a worthy person; rājasic giving expects return; tāmasic giving is done with contempt in wrong circumstances
nodes.repetition_rationale: The three-guna analysis of dāna is unique to ch17; emphasizes context (deśa-kāla-pātra) as constitutive of the quality of giving
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for dānaṃ + deśa + kāla + pātra — this tetrad appears in dharmaśāstra and is echoed in Anuśāsanaparva chapters on dānadharma

nodes.name: OM TAT SAT — Three Designations of Brahman
nodes.proposition: OM, TAT, SAT are the three-fold designation of Brahman from which all rites, Vedas, and brahmins were instituted; actions without śraddhā are called asat and are naught in this world or the next
nodes.repetition_rationale: Unique ritual-ontological node: OM TAT SAT as three names of Brahman; the chapter ends with the negative — action without śraddhā is asat, real neither here nor hereafter
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for oṃ tat sad iti nirdeśo — this exact phrase; also aśraddhayā hutaṃ dattaṃ as the negative marker; track in ritual and Brahman-naming contexts across MBH

nodes.name: Sannyasa vs Tyaga — Resolution of the Distinction
nodes.proposition: Sannyāsa is the abandonment of desire-motivated actions; tyāga is the abandonment of the fruit of all actions; the tyāgī performs yajña-dāna-tapas without attachment to fruit — this is sattvic tyāga
nodes.repetition_rationale: Ch18 opens with the question Arjuna posed at the start — the BG's entire arc resolves here; this node is the final answer to N10 (tyāga vs sannyāsa), now with a clear verdict: sattvic tyāga = perform duty, drop the fruit
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for kāmyānāṃ karmaṇāṃ nyāsaṃ + sarvakarmaphalatyāgam — the technical pairing of sannyāsa and tyāga definitions; also phalatyāgī as a descriptor in MBH ethical passages

nodes.name: Five Causes of All Action — The Samkhya Account
nodes.proposition: All actions are accomplished by five causes: the locus (body), the agent, the instruments, the various operations, and the fifth — fate (daiva); one who sees only the self as agent is of perverted understanding
nodes.repetition_rationale: Unique Sāṃkhya-inflected analysis of action causation; the five-cause theory distributes agency and undoes the ego's claim to sole authorship; daiva as the fifth cause is crucial
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for pañcaitāni kāraṇāni + adhiṣṭhānaṃ tathā kartā — this exact enumeration; Sāṃkhya discussions in Śāntiparva on the causes of action will echo this

nodes.name: Knowledge Karma Karta in Three Gunas
nodes.proposition: Knowledge, action, and the agent are each of three kinds according to the gunas: sattvic knowledge sees one imperishable in all; sattvic action is obligatory and non-attached; the sattvic doer is free of ego and fortitude
nodes.repetition_rationale: The triplication of jñāna-karma-kartā by gunas is ch18's systematic exposition; it operationalizes the guna framework (N06) into the three fundamental categories of every action
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for jñānaṃ karma ca kartā ca tridhaiva guṇabhedataḥ — this exact formula or its paraphrase in Sāṃkhya-Yoga expositions; also muktasaṅgo 'nahaṃvādī as a quality-descriptor

nodes.name: Buddhi and Dhrti in Three Gunas
nodes.proposition: Intellect (buddhi) that knows pravṛtti and nivṛtti, what is to be done and not done, bondage and liberation — that is sattvic; dhṛti that maintains mind, prāṇa, and senses by yoga without wavering — that is sattvic
nodes.repetition_rationale: Unique to ch18 — the application of the three-guna framework to the faculties of intellect and resolve; distinct from knowledge or action, these are the meta-capacities that govern how knowledge and action unfold
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for pravṛttiṃ ca nivṛttiṃ ca kāryākārye + bandhaṃ mokṣaṃ — this tetrad of what buddhi must discern; also dhṛtyā dhārayate manaḥprāṇendriyakriyāḥ

nodes.name: Three Types of Pleasure — Sattvic Pleasure Through Practice
nodes.proposition: Sattvic pleasure is like poison at first, nectar at the end — born of the clarity of self-knowledge through practice; rājasic is nectar at first and poison at the end; tāmasic deludes from beginning to end
nodes.repetition_rationale: The only explicit discussion of pleasure's quality by guna — the paradox that real happiness is hard at first; this reverses ordinary preference and gives a criterion for discriminating practice
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for viṣam iva pariṇāme 'mṛtopamam — the poison-nectar reversal metaphor for sattvic pleasure; also ātmabuddhiprasādajaṃ as the source of authentic happiness

nodes.name: Varnashrama as Nature-Born — Svabhava Prabhava
nodes.proposition: The duties of brāhmaṇa, kṣatriya, vaiśya, and śūdra are distributed according to their own nature-born qualities; by devotion to one's own duty one attains perfection
nodes.repetition_rationale: This node extends N13 (svadharma) by grounding it in svabhāva — one's nature-born guna configuration determines one's proper field of action; perfection is available to each through one's own domain
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for svabhāvaprabhavair guṇaiḥ + karmāṇi pravibhaktāni — the nature-basis for duty-distribution; also svakarmaṇā tam abhyarcya + siddhim — worship through one's own work as path to perfection

nodes.name: Naiskarmasiddhi — Perfection Through Non-Action
nodes.proposition: With unattached intellect, victorious self, gone beyond desire, one attains through renunciation the supreme perfection of non-action (naiṣkarmya)
nodes.repetition_rationale: The culminating practical path before the highest devotional teaching — naiṣkarmyasiddhi is the Vedantic goal described through the qualities of the liberated intellect; this node bridges the action discussion with the brahma-bhūta state
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for naiṣkarmyasiddhiṃ paramāṃ + saṃnyāsenādhigacchati — the phrase is rare and specific; also asaktabuddhi + sarvatra + jitātmā as a descriptor cluster for the renunciant

nodes.name: Brahma-Bhuta — The State of Becoming Brahman
nodes.proposition: Having become Brahman, one is serene, neither grieving nor craving, equal to all beings — in that state one attains supreme devotion to Me
nodes.repetition_rationale: The brahma-bhūta state is the pivot — not the end but the platform from which supreme bhakti arises; the sequence brahma-bhūta → parā bhakti → tattvaṃ jñātvā → viśate is the BG's final soteriological map
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for brahmabhūtaḥ prasannātmā + na śocati na kāṅkṣati + samaḥ sarvabhūteṣu — this triple is a complete formula; any MBH sage described in these terms is echoing this node

nodes.name: Buddhi Yoga — Surrendering All Actions to Krishna
nodes.proposition: Fix the mind on Me, take refuge in Me by buddhi-yoga; surrendering all actions to Me with mind on Me, you will cross all difficulties by My grace — if ego prevents, nature will compel
nodes.repetition_rationale: This node restates N11 (yoga as equanimity in action) but now in its most personal and theistic register — mat-para, mat-citta, mat-prasāda; also adds the counter-claim: if ego refuses, prakṛti will enforce anyway
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for cetasā sarvakarmāṇi mayi saṃnyasya + buddhiyogam — the combination of surrendering all actions with buddhi-yoga; also prakṛtis tvāṃ niyokṣyati as a statement about nature's compulsion

nodes.name: Ishvara as the Mover — Yantrarudhani Mayaya
nodes.proposition: The Lord dwells in the heart of all beings, causing all beings to revolve as if mounted on a machine by māyā — take refuge in Him alone with all your being
nodes.repetition_rationale: Refines N31 (Ishvara as inner ruler) by adding the mechanical metaphor — beings are puppets on a machine, moved by māyā; the response to this vision is total surrender (zaraNam), leading directly into 18.66
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for yantrārūḍhāni māyayā + bhrāmayan — this mechanical metaphor is striking; also hṛddeśe 'rjuna tiṣṭhati as a statement of divine immanence in the heart

nodes.name: Sarva-Dharman Parityajya — The Final Teaching
nodes.proposition: Abandon all dharmas and take refuge in Me alone — I will liberate you from all sins; do not grieve
nodes.repetition_rationale: The caramopadeśa — the final secret teaching; alone in the BG as a direct divine promise of liberation; distinct from N17 (which is the thematic node) because this node is anchored in the single verse as the BG's irreducible core
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for sarvadharmān parityajya + mām ekaṃ śaraṇaṃ vraja — this is one of the most-quoted BG verses; its echo in MBH would be any statement where a god or sage promises liberation to one who takes total refuge

nodes.name: Gita as Sacred Teaching — Transmission and Reception
nodes.proposition: This Gītā should not be taught to one who has no austerity, devotion, or who finds fault; one who teaches it to devotees renders the highest service and will come to Me; one who merely hears it with faith is liberated
nodes.repetition_rationale: The Gītā-māhātmya (praise and transmission rules) frames the teaching as self-referentially sacred; the BG is itself a node in the MBH — this node marks the meta-level of the BG-within-MBH structure
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for idaṃ te nātapaskāya — the restriction formula; also jñānayajñena + iṣṭaḥ syām as the frame of the Gītā as a jñāna-sacrifice; the BG's self-designation matters for the Ekam Sat project

nodes.name: Sanjaya's Witness — The BG as a Reported Event
nodes.proposition: Sañjaya reports: I heard this wondrous dialogue between Vāsudeva and Pārtha through Vyāsa's grace; wherever Yoga's Lord and Arjuna the archer are, there is prosperity, victory, and right conduct
nodes.repetition_rationale: The BG is embedded in the MBH through Sañjaya — this narrative frame node is essential for the Ekam Sat project: the BG is not a separate text but a discourse within the MBH, reported by a witness to Dhṛtarāṣṭra
nodes.echo_hypothesis: Search for yogeśvaraḥ kṛṣṇo yatra pārtho dhanurdharaḥ — the closing śloka is itself echoed and quoted in MBH; also sañjaya uvāca + saMvAdam as the embedded-discourse framing device
